this is why i wrote a post on X earlier that i don't like that many plp try to oversimplify/interpret lvls simply as support or resistance lvls.

comment.body:
as said, when you buy an option, you buy vol. 
you bet on or against the IV on it.
what is vol? the SD of the logreturns.
what does it mean? how much SD is expected for the price action. 
as I showed in the post about vol, this assumption is based on gaussian distribution, or normal distribution (same concept).
it is symmetrical, every sigma values have a probability values.

we can take the IV of a contract, multiply it by 1/sqrt(252) and so it reveals us the 1 sigma range that the market bets on for that contract alone. 
the common practice is to take the ATM IV on lets say weekly expo.
some use the ruole of 16, bcs sqrt(252) is close to 16... "good enough"
my calculation is a bit more complex, but the idea is to take the ranges that the market bets on

comment.body: Alma, one part I don't get yet: Typically MM hedging is explained that initially they trade the underlying asset by the -delta value of the option, and opposite on expiration/closure. This would mean asset volume = volume_implied_by_customers + volume_by_continuous_gamma_hedging + volume_by_initial_and_final_delta_neutralization. With the latter one would be quite high during expiration dates. Translated into gamma world, it would mean we would see gamma spikes during option selling and massive gamma spikes during expiration. Until now I didn't see this effect being modeled in the net gamma, but the effect should be significant? So is above a too naive model of MM hedging? How do they really reverse their hedge at expiration date?
comment.raw_body: Alma, one part I don't get yet: Typically MM hedging is explained that initially they trade the underlying asset by the -delta value of the option, and opposite on expiration/closure. This would mean asset volume = volume_implied_by_customers + volume_by_continuous_gamma_hedging + volume_by_initial_and_final_delta_neutralization. With the latter one would be quite high during expiration dates. Translated into gamma world, it would mean we would see gamma spikes during option selling and massive gamma spikes during expiration. Until now I didn't see this effect being modeled in the net gamma, but the effect should be significant? So is above a too naive model of MM hedging? How do they really reverse their hedge at expiration date?

comment.body:
just to make it clear for everyone. When I'm talking about risk, i talk about this: 
Lets say the both of these start and end points are the same starting and ending values . So you can go from A to B with lower risk, lower SD, and higher risk higher SD.
this is why idon't take longer term position. AMD ER is coming it will be likely fomo, and that will create big vol

comment.body:
tbh i'm not sure i understand the question well (language limits), but:
hedging is a continuous process to stay as delta-neutral as possible, dynamic hedging is not a binary “start–reverse” process. Also MMs can use options, correlated instruments to hedge gamma risk.
comment.raw_body:
tbh i'm not sure i understand the question well (language limits), but:
hedging is a continuous process to stay as delta-neutral as possible, dynamic hedging is not a binary “start–reverse” process. Also MMs can use options, correlated instruments to hedge gamma risk.

comment.body: Alma, one part I don't get yet: Typically MM hedging is explained that initially they trade the underlying asset by the -delta value of the option, and opposite on expiration/closure. This would mean asset volume = volume_implied_by_customers + volume_by_continuous_gamma_hedging + volume_by_initial_and_final_delta_neutralization. With the latter one would be quite high during expiration dates. Translated into gamma world, it would mean we would see gamma spikes during option selling and massive gamma spikes during expiration. Until now I didn't see this effect being modeled in the net gamma, but the effect should be significant? So is above a too naive model of MM hedging? How do they really reverse their hedge at expiration date?

This would mean asset volume = volume_implied_by_customers + volume_by_continuous_gamma_hedging + volume_by_initial_and_final_delta_neutralization. With the latter one would be quite high during expiration dates. Translated into gamma world, it would mean we would see gamma spikes during option selling and massive gamma spikes during expiration. comment.body: adn don't forget that they hedge a complex portfolio. so aggregate gamma is smoother than what one sees in a single-option model

user.name: Alma

comment.body:
also MMs avoid executing one large, end-of-day reversal.
instead of this, they unwind their hedges gradually during the session so they prevent a sudden spike in trading volume, bcs the unwinding process is spread over time
comment.raw_body:
